text: {"matches":[{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 1 gives a specific protein amount for a whole egg, which is a concrete instance of the general statement in claim 0 that eggs contain significant protein."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":4,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 4 describes the composition of egg white, while claim 0 refers to yolk and whole egg protein/choline; they address different egg parts."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Claim 3 provides grams and fatty‑acid percentages of a whole egg, whereas claim 2 discusses the proportion of calories derived from yolk fat; both concern fat but use different metrics."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":5,"toIndex":9,"rationale":"Claim 5 reports that dietary cholesterol raises the total‑to‑HDL cholesterol ratio, while claim 9 reports that eggs raise total and LDL cholesterol; the outcomes measured differ."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":10,"rationale":"Both claims state that moderate egg consumption does not increase cardiovascular disease risk; claim 6 limits the population to healthy people, creating a slight scope difference."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":7,"toIndex":8,"rationale":"Claim 7 links higher egg intake to reduced coronary artery disease risk, whereas claim 8 links egg intake to increased cardiovascular disease mortality—opposite directions for related outcomes."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":9,"toIndex":10,"rationale":"Claim 9 shows eggs raise total and LDL cholesterol, while claim 10 concludes no increased cardiovascular disease risk; the former is a biochemical effect, the latter a clinical outcome."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":5,"toIndex":10,"rationale":"Claim 5 concerns the cholesterol ratio effect of dietary cholesterol, whereas claim 10 addresses overall cardiovascular disease risk; they pertain to different outcome domains."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":7,"rationale":"Claim 6 says egg consumption does not increase heart disease risk, while claim 7 reports a reduction in coronary artery disease risk; both are risk‑related but refer to different specific outcomes."}]}
